title: char, Rune и UTF-16: code unit против code point

cards.prompt: A char is a 16-bit UTF-16 code unit: the ox emoji U+1F402 is a supplementary code point that needs two char instances so string.Length is 2, but it is a single Unicode scalar value so EnumerateRunes counts 1, printing 2 then 1
cards.code: using System; using System.Linq; using System.Text; string s = "\U0001F402"; Console.WriteLine(s.Length); Console.WriteLine(s.EnumerateRunes().Count());

cards.prompt: string.Length counts UTF-16 code units, not characters: "a" + ox emoji (two chars) + "b" is 4 chars but 3 Unicode scalar values (a, emoji, b), so Length is 4 and EnumerateRunes is 3, printing 4 then 3
cards.code: using System; using System.Linq; using System.Text; string s = "a\U0001F402b"; Console.WriteLine(s.Length); Console.WriteLine(s.EnumerateRunes().Count());

cards.prompt: The two chars of the ox emoji are a surrogate pair: s[0] is a high surrogate (True), s[1] is a low surrogate (True), and char.ConvertToUtf32 reassembles them into the supplementary code point U+1F402 = 128002, printing True True 128002
